titleStmt.title: Ivan Langstroth
born Alameda, CA, October 16, 1887; died New York City April 18, 1971

publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.head: Career Summary
div.p: Ivan Langstroth received musical training in San Francisco and then at the Berlin Hochschule für Musik. He studied piano with Josef Levinne and composition with Engelbert Humperdinck. In 1915 he became a coach at the Kiel Opera, and in 1916 organist at the American Church in Berlin. Between 1917 and 1920 he toured Scandinavia as a concert pianist, after which he settled in Vienna, where he taught composition and theory at the Neues Wiener Konservatorium from 1921 to 1928. Returning to the USA, he taught at Chatham Square Music School, City College, and Brooklyn College in New York City, where he died in 1971
He composed one symphony, a piano concerto (described in Schenker’s diary for February 10, 1927), chamber music, piano music, organ music, solo vocal and choral music.

div.head: Langstroth and Schenker
div.p: Langstroth first approached Schenker on November 5, 1922: diary: “asks for lessons; only I can save him, studied with Humperdink, Kaun, Juon, but didn't learn anything.” He was already known to Carl Bamberger. He attended his first lesson on March 2, 1923 and his last sometime in in the following month, with unknown frequency.

listBibl.bibl: New York Public Library, Archives (scores and personal papers)
University of California, Berkeley, Hargrove Library, Archives (scores and correspondence)
